documentTitle: LLM: How Large Language Models Think
lede: Large Language Models like GPT and Claude learn to understand and generate text by processing billions of words. Let's trace a prompt from raw text to model-generated response — step by step.
attentionNote: The model weighs relationships between tokens:

steps.title: The user types a prompt
steps.desc: You type a question or instruction into the chatbot. This raw text is the starting point — the model can't read words directly. It needs a numerical representation first.

steps.title: Tokenization: Breaking text into tokens
steps.desc: The text is split into small pieces called 'tokens'. Each token represents a word, sub-word, or character. The model has a fixed vocabulary — each recognized token is matched to a unique number (token ID). Unknown words are broken into smaller sub-tokens.

steps.title: Embedding: Converting tokens to vectors
steps.desc: Each token ID is converted into a high-dimensional numerical vector (e.g. 4096 dimensions). This vector captures the semantic meaning of the token — words with similar meanings end up close together in vector space.

steps.title: Self-Attention: Understanding token relationships
steps.desc: This is the core of the Transformer architecture. Each token 'attends to' other tokens to understand context. For example, the token 'is' attends to 'LLM' to understand what the sentence is about. The Query-Key-Value mechanism computes relevance scores between all token pairs in parallel.

steps.title: Decoder: Generating the predicted token
steps.desc: After passing through many Transformer layers, the model outputs a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary. The token with the highest probability (or one chosen via sampling strategy) becomes the next word in the response. This process repeats token by token until the response is complete.
fields: LLM stands for Large Language Model
